text: Clouds act as a medium for heterogeneous interaction of gas-phase and aerosol constituents and therefore strongly influence the aerosol composition. This study determines cloud water atmospheric organic matter (AOM) composition and cloud processing of aerosols, based on two cloud samples collected at the Storm Peak Laboratory near Steamboat Springs, Colorado (3220 m a.s.l.). A reversed-phase extraction (RPE) method was used to isolate the AOM from cloud water samples. The resulting ultrahigh-resolution mass spectra contained over 3000 molecular formulas. These consisted of nitrogen-containing compounds (CHNO), sulfur-containing compounds (CHOS and CHNOS), and other oxygen-containing compounds (CHO), and had molecular weights up to 700 Da. The oxygen-to-carbon ratio for the cloud water AOM samples was approximately 0.6, which is slightly higher than that for the majority of the water-soluble organic carbon (WSOC) components observed in aerosol studies. Possible causes include aqueous oxidation within clouds. The composition of the cloud water AOM indicated a strong contribution of biogenic secondary organic aerosol (SOA) and residential wood combustion. The number of formulas with carbon numbers ≥20 corresponding to known SOA products was large (60% of total CHO formulas), which included the products of α-pinene, β-pinene, d-limonene, and β-caryophyllene ozonolysis. The abundance of CHNO formulas indicated a large contribution from residential wood combustion, as well as from NOx oxidation. In addition, the nighttime cloud sample composite had formulas with multiple nitrogen atoms, confirming the role of nitrate radical reactions on the cloud water AOM. A few formulas belonged to the class of sulfur-containing compounds (CHOS and CHNOS), and contained reduced sulfur (in addition to the more commonly observed oxidized sulfur-containing compounds), however further investigations are needed to clarify the causes of the reduced sulfur-containing compounds. In summary, using the ultrahigh-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FT-ICR) mass spectrometry, this study obtained an unambiguous identification of the cloud water organic anion composition in the Rocky Mountain area and is useful for the understanding of aqueous-phase processes.
